coredata.dc:title: Continuous synthesis of N, S co-coped carbon dots for selective detection of CD (II) ions
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Cheng, Yu
Chen, Zhuo
Wang, Yundong
Xu, Jianhong
coredata.dc:description: Despite the rising interest in carbon dots (CDs) for applications as nano sensors, rapid and reproducible synthesis of CDs is a crucial problem forscalable production and practical application. The microreactor system was applied in this work for continuous synthesis of functionalized carbon dots with high monodispersity. Nitrogen and sulfur co-coped CDs were produced from L-cysteine and citric acid through coil microreactor in 10 min. The obtained N, S co-coped CDs had a shape of nearly sphere with an average diameter of 2.8 nm. The CDs exhibited bright blue fluorescence when excited upon ultraviolet light, reaching a high quantum yield at 68.2%. The fluorescence could be selectively quenched by Cd2+ and were demonstrated suitable for Cd2+ detection with high sensitivity and excellent selectivity. In this study, the N, S co-coped CDs reached the detection limit of 0.079 ppb within a wide linear range from 0 ppb to 300 ppb. In addition, this detection method held a strong anti-interference ability in complex ionic environments and was demonstrated low cell-toxicity, indicating promising applications in Cd2+ detection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Microreactor
Cd2+ detection
Fluorescence detection
Nanosensor

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Continuous synthesis system of carbon dots

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Illustration of continuous synthesis system of CDs and the picture of TEM image (inserted a picture of CDs solution compared in sun light and UV light).
(a) HRTEM image of the synthesized N&S CDs; (b) particle size distribution of CDs and fitting of normal distribution.
(a) XPS scan spectrum of CDs and high-resolution XPS spectra of (b) C1s, (c) N1s, (d) O1s, (e) S2p and (f) FTIR spectra of CDs.
(a)UV–Vis absorbance spectrum of CDs. (b) Photoluminescence spectra at varied excitation wavelength ranging from 320 to 400 nm.

Stability of CDs after different treatments. (a)Intensity variation of CDs solution after UV exposure for different time. (b) Intensity variation of CDs solution at room temperature for different time. (c) Intensity variation of CDs solution in different concentration of salt. (d) Emission variation of CDs solution in environment of different pH. (e) Emission wavelength variation and (f) intensity variation of CDs solution in environment of different pH. F0 and F represent the fluorescent intensity of CDs at emission wavelengthof 425 nm excited at 365 nm before and after the treatment, respectively.

(a) Fluorescent response of CDs to different metal ions in aqueous solution. 1 ppm (mg/L) was used for each metal ions. F0 and F represented the fluorescent intensity of emission at emission wavelengthof 425 nm excited at 365 nm without and with the ions, respectively. (b) PL emission spectrum of CDs upon addition of various concentrations of Cd2+ at an excitation wavelength of 365 nm.
(a) The quantitative relationship of F0/Fagainst the Cd2+ concentration over the range of0–300 ppb. (b) Photographs of CDs solution with Cd2+ concentrations of from 0 to 10 ppm.

(a) UV–Vis absorption spectra of the CDs (black line) and CDs after exposing to Cd2+ ions (red line). (b) The Zeta potential of CDs before and after addition of Cd2+ ions. (c) Lifetime decays of CDs before and after addition of Cd2+ ions.
The comparison among previously reported methods of Cd2+ ions detection.
Cd2+ ions detection in real water samples.

head.ce:title: Continuous synthesis of N, S co-coped carbon dots for selective detection of CD (II) ions

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: The State Key Laboratory of Chemical Engineering, Department of Chemical Engineering, Tsinghua University, Beijing 100084, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The State Key Laboratory of Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: The State Key Laboratory of Chemical Engineering, Department of Chemical Engineering, Tsinghua University, Beijing 100084, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Despite the rising interest in carbon dots (CDs) for applications as nano sensors, rapid and reproducible synthesis of CDs is a crucial problem forscalable production and practical application. The microreactor system was applied in this work for continuous synthesis of functionalized carbon dots with high monodispersity. Nitrogen and sulfur co-coped CDs were produced from L-cysteine and citric acid through coil microreactor in 10 min. The obtained N, S co-coped CDs had a shape of nearly sphere with an average diameter of 2.8 nm. The CDs exhibited bright blue fluorescence when excited upon ultraviolet light, reaching a high quantum yield at 68.2%. The fluorescence could be selectively quenched by Cd2+ and were demonstrated suitable for Cd2+ detection with high sensitivity and excellent selectivity. In this study, the N, S co-coped CDs reached the detection limit of 0.079 ppb within a wide linear range from 0 ppb to 300 ppb. In addition, this detection method held a strong anti-interference ability in complex ionic environments and was demonstrated low cell-toxicity, indicating promising applications in Cd2+ detection.

ce:section.ce:para: Heavy metal ions released into the environment are not biodegradable and accumulation of these ions has caused wide pollution threatening human health and ecosystem [1,2]. Especially, cadmium ion (Cd2+) has been widely used in aerospace, battery technology, metallurgy electroplating and agriculture [3,4] and it is considered one of the most poisonous metal ions. Cadmium exposure could increase the risk of several cancers such as prostate, lungs, bones, and abdominal cancer [5,6]. Different world organizations such as the World Health Organization (WHO) have regulated the maximum allowable level of Cd2+ in the environment [7]. Therefore, accurate and rapid monitoring of Cd2+ pollution is highly desired to ensure public health and environment safety. Generally, several kinds of quantitative analysis methods have been used for Cd2+ detection, including atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS) [8,9], inductively coupled plasma-mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) [10,11], and so on. These methods exhibit advantages of high sensitivity and low limits of detection (LOD) but usually require expensive instrument and complex operations.

In recent years, fluorescence (FL) sensors have been developed for detection of various toxic metal ions due to their high sensitivity, cost-effective property, and real-time detection ability [12-14]. Carbon dots (CDs) have been widely used as fluorescent probe because of their excellent optical properties such as high brightness, and multiplexing ability. Comparing to organic dyes and traditional quantum dots, CDs have drawn great attention for good solubility, outstanding biocompatibility, low toxicity and excellent stability in variable environment [15-17]. Noticeably, the doping strategy was developed to regulate the photophysical properties, where heteroatoms or groups are combined with carbon skeleton in CDs [18].Within doping operation, not only the fluorescence efficiency can be improved, new functionalities can be imparted to the CDs. Yang’s group [19] applied response surface methodology to produce N-doped CDs for Cd2+ detection. Xu et al. [20] prepared S-doped CDs applied as probes towards Fe3+ detection.

Approaches for synthesizing CDs are divided into top-down and bottom-up methods. Top-down methods, where reagents are bulk carbon materials, are usually used for perfectly crystalized carbon quantum dots, such as chemical ablation [21], laser ablation [22], and arc discharge [23]. Bottom-up methods, starting from small molecules, permit regulation of the structure and composition of CDs through optimization of recants and reaction conditions, including carbonization process [24], template method [25], hydrothermal/solvothermal synthesis [26] and so on. These methods usually involved batch reaction progress for several hours under harsh condition, accompanied by significant time consumption and poor reproducibility between batches. Especially, differentiation between batches could inevitably lead to unexpected deviations in detection result. Therefore, approaches for rapid synthesis of homogenous CDs are required for accurate analyzation. The continuous reaction system based on microfluidic technology can greatly enhance the mixing efficiency and reduce reaction time from hours to several minutes by virtue of excellent heat and mass transfer performance. These routes usually involve one-step solvothermal progress, in which one or more strands are rapidly mixed in microreactors. This method has been applied successfully in production of various materials, including metal oxides [27],nanometals [28],2D materials [29], nanocomposites [30] and N-doped CDs [31]. Generally, this method could avoid the uncontrollability of traditional batch processes and be transformed for scaled production of nanomaterials such as CDs, giving high quality and reproducibility for a homogenous product with optimized properties.

In this work, L-cystine and citric acid were used as precursors and N, S co-coped carbon dots are produced through continuous hydrothermal process in microreactor system within 10 min. The prepared CDs exhibited blue fluorescence upon excitation of ultraviolet light, reaching a high quantum yield at 68.2%. The fluorescence was stable in complex environment and selectively quenched by Cd2+, which allows the CDs to be used as sensors towards Cd2+ ions in aqueous solution with a wide concentration range of 0–300 ppb and the detection limit of 0.079 ppb.

L-cysteine (99%) and citric acid (99%) were purchased from Beijing J&K Scientific Corporation. All metal salts used in the experiment were of analytical grade. The used deionized water was prepared from laboratory water purification system produced by the lab Instrument and Technology Corporation with a resistivity higher than 18.2 MΩ·cm−1.

The ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectra were captured on UV-2450 from Shimadzu Corporation. Fourier Transform InfraRed (FT-IR) spectra were recorded with a Nicolet 6700 FT-IR spectrometer. The high-resolutiontransmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images of carbon dots were obtained on a JEOL JEM-2010 (Japan) microscope operated at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was measured using a Thermo Fisher ESCALAB 250Xi spectrometer. Zeta potential of CDs in water solution was measured by SOE-070. Lifetime of products were measured trough the DeltaFlex time correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) system from HORIBA. The quantum yield was measured with the Nanolog fluorescence spectrometer from HORIBA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Continuous synthesis system of carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 1 , continuous synthesis system consisted of three parts: a feeding section, a high-temperature reaction section and a condensation section. A back pressure valve (BPV) was set after the cooler to regulate the pressure of reaction section at 4.5 Mpa. Citric acid (CA) and L-cystine were dissolved in deionized water at the concentration of 50 g/L. Then the solution was pumped into coil microreactor under oil bath with temperature of 180 °C. Noticeably, the volume of reactor was 40 ml and the pump flow rate was set as 4 ml/min, determining the reaction time at 10 min. CA decomposed into carbon dioxide and water at the reaction temperature, resulting in gas–liquid segmented flow in the micro-channels and enhancing mixing efficiency advantageous to reaction. As a result, carbon dots were generated in coil reactor. In this case, the residence time distribution could be much narrower for synthesizing monodispersed CDs. The products went through the cooler and were collected after BPV. The obtained liquid was centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 10 min to remove large particles. Finally, the transparent supernatant was dialyzed against distilled water in a dialysis bag (1000 Da) for 12 h and high-quality carbon dots were fabricated. The related apparatus and corresponding optimization of the preparation conditions are shown in Fig. S1.

All detection experiments were carried out at room temperature. Under single excitation condition (λex = 365 nm), the CDs exhibit bright fluorescence at 425 nm. Then solution of ions with different concentrations and solution of CDs with fixed concentration were mixed at certain proportion, and shaken until intensive mixing. As a typical process of detection for Cd2+, 1.000 ml aqueous solution of carbon dots were mixed with × ml solution of CdCl2 at 1 ppm (mg/L) and then diluted to 4.000 ml (the × could be changeable for required concentration in final solution). Then after sufficient shaken, fluorescence intensity of the mixture was measured at the wavelength of 425 nm with excitation wavelength at 365 nm. The experiments were repeated three times for each group to ensure the stability of the detection process. As for real water sample experiment, CdCl2 was dissolved into the real water sample, and the other steps were controlled the same.

The morphology and structureof CDs were characterized by TEM in Fig. 2 . The observed CDs were well separated from each other without apparent aggregation, exhibiting a shape of nearly sphere with a narrow diameter distribution of 2.4 nm − 3.4 nm at average of 2.8 nm. No obvious lattice fringe was observed in TEM image, indicating an amorphous carbon skeleton structure [32].

The XPS spectra provide information of electronic states and chemical composition of the CDs. The XPS spectrum of the CDs exhibits four significant peaks belonging toC1s, O1s, N1s, and S2p, as given in Fig. 3 . Calculated from spectral peak area, the percentage of C, O, N, and S is 56.00%, 7.67%, 30.96% and 5.36%, respectively. In high-resolution XPS spectra, carbon atoms exhibit a diverse chemical environment. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of the C1s band can be separated into four peaks at 284.78 eV, 286.50 eV, 288.17 eV and 288.83 eV, which are assigned to C=C/C–C, C–O, C–S, and C=O/C=N, respectively. The N1s XPS spectrum shows three peaks at 399.13 eV, 399.94 eV and 400.35 eV, originated from C–N–C, N=C and N–H, respectively. The O1s XPS spectrum exhibits two typical peaks corresponding to O=C at 531.47 eV and O–H at 532.95 eV. The S2p spectrum of the CDs can be fitted into three peaks. The two peaks at 164.62 eV and 165.00 eV can be assigned to S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2. Another peak at 163.42 eV can be ascribed to S-H, retaining the typical structure of L-cysteine after complete doping of these precursors [33,34]. FTIR absorption were further measured to explore the composition and structure of CDs in Fig. 3(f). The peak located at 631 cm−1 is attributed to the existence of C-S bonds. The peak captured at 1712 cm−1 originates from C=O bonds. These peaks are ascribed to the reactants, citric acid and L-cysteine. Meanwhile, the inclusion of S=O bonds contribute to the peak at 1024 cm−1. The peak at 1402 cm−1 arises from C=S bonds. The peak captured at 1663 cm−1 originates from the formation of C=C bonds. The absorption peak at 3196 cm−1 exhibits the generation of amide structure. These evidences indicate retainment of original functional groups and occurrence of complex polymerization reactions. In addition, peaks at 2933 cm−1 and 2928 cm−1 are ascribed to –OH and –COOH group, explaining the excellent water solubility of CDs.

As shown in Fig. 4 , UV–Vis spectrum exhibits two bands around 348 nm and 289 nm, ascribing to π-π ∗ transition and n-π ∗ transition of sp2 aromatic carbon, demonstrating a conjugated structure in core. PL spectrum demonstrates the observation of fluorescence under different excitation wavelength from 320 nm to 400 nm, with the strongest emission under excitation at 350 nm. The emission wavelength is located at 425 nm, almost independent of excitation light. The excitation wavelength-independent emission properties can be attributed to high normality of CDs. The quantum yield (QY) of CDs was measured at 68.2%, far exceeding those of previous reported sulfur or nitrogen doped CDs [35-39].

The stability of CDs in different environments was investigated to ensure the versatility. First, the solution of CDs was exposed to UV light for 4 h or daylight lamp at room temperature for one month. As shown in Fig. 5 (a) and (b), no obvious fluorescence quenching was observed after UV light exposure or long-time room-temperature storage. This result showed the good photostability of CDs, promising the availability even after unproper storage. Furthermore, the behavior of CDs in various aqueous environments was evaluated. Solution of CDs was mixed with NaCl salt in different concentration. As shown in Fig. 5(c), the intensity of CDs was almost steady while salt concentration arising from 10 μM to 500 μM, suggesting potential application in severe ionic environment. The effect of CD solution pH ranging from 2 to 12 on the FL emission intensity was also evaluated as shown in Fig. 5(d–f). The pH value of the solution was fitted by adding 0.1 M HCl and 0.1 M NaOH. Emission wavelength was located at 425 nm for most conditions (pH = 5–12), and red shift of 20 nm was observed under strong soil environment (pH = 2–4). Noticeably, fluorescence emission was significantly quenched under extremely acidic environment. This change is supposed to be caused by the destruction of conjugated luminescence structure by amino protonation on carbon surface under acidic environment. Except for extreme environment mentioned above, emission intensity was almost steady under most conditions, indicating the application value of CDs under a wide environment.

In order to evaluate the selectivity and specificity of CDs as a fluorescent probe for cadmium ions, the fluorescence intensity of different metal ions, such as Fe3+, Fe2+, Cr3+, Pb2+, Ca2+, Mg2+, K+, Na+, Ag+, in the presence of 1 ppm, was analyzed. Quenching effect of the fluorescence intensity (F/F0, F and F0 represent the FL intensity of CDs with and without metal ions) at 425 nm with excitation at 365 nm was measured through same process. As shown in Fig. 6 (a), only Cd2+ gave a significant quenching effect for fluorescence, while fluorescence quenching efficiency of other metal ions did not exceed 10% at the ion concentration of 1 ppm. Addition of Cd2+ reduced the intensity efficiency to nearly 60%. Pb2+ ions could be the strongest interference in which a quenching efficiency of about 8 % was observed, probably resulting from the potential interaction of Pb2+ and sulfur-related groups on the surface. Fluorescence variation of CDs accompanied by concentration of Cd2+ was depicted in Fig. 6(b). Cd2+ solution with different concentration from 0 − 10 ppm was used to evaluate this detection method. At excitation wavelength of 365 nm, the fluorescence intensity of CDs gradually declined with an increase in Cd2+ concentration, which suggested the effective quenching ability of Cd2+.

Fluorescence quenching of CDs solution highly depended on the concentration of Cd2+, which built the basic of quantitative detection of Cd2+. According to Stern-Volmer equation, a well-fitted linear relationship between luminescence intensity and concentration was observed from 0 ppb to 300 ppb. As shown in Fig. 7 , linear regression between F/F0(Y) and Cd2+(X) could be obtained as Y = 6.259E-4*X + 1(R2 = 0.99997). The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 0.079 ppb as per 3σ/k (where σ and k denote the standard deviation of the intercept and slope of the calibration curve, respectively). This is comparable to the formerly reported values shown in Table 1 .

In addition, the potential of our detection method in real cases was investigated. In this part, the Cd2+ ions were measured and prepared as samples in three levels, including 50 ppb, 100 ppb and 200 ppb, which were further spiked into real samples such as tap water and river water. As comparison to our methods, the ICP analyses of the actual water samples were conducted. With regard to the results of this experiment in Table 2 , our strategy was revealed a high degree of accuracy and convenience for detection of Cd2+ in real water sample. Furthermore, CDs was proved with low cytotoxicity, indicating the safety of practice detection, as shown in Fig. S2.
To explore the mechanism of Cd2+ detection, different characterization methods was applied to examine the changes of properties of CDs before and after the addition of ions.

Stern-Volmer equation could be used to analyze the quenching process. Usually in fluorescence quenching process,
F 0
/ F = 1 +
K
SV
Q
= 1 +
K q
τ 0
Q
where F0 and Fwere the fluorescence intensities without and with the addition of quencher, respectively. In this case, K SV is the Stern–Volmer constant, [Q] is the concentration of Cd2+, and τ 0 is the lifetime of CDs before addition of quencher. Under room temperature, KSV was 70090(L/mol). Meanwhile, τ 0 was measured as 10.78 ns through time-correlated single-photon counting (TCSPC), which means Kq was 6.5 × 1012 L·mol−1·s−1. A bigger constant suggests a static quenching process, while a typical dynamic quenching rate constant does not usually exceed 2 × 1010 L·mol−1·s−1 [41,47].

As shown in Fig. 8 (a), the absorption peak of pure CDs is centered at 348 nm. After addition of Cd2+, the original peak almost disappeared while a new peak at around 300 nm has arisen. Because dynamic quenching only affects the excited state of fluorescent molecules and has no effect on ultraviolet absorption, this result supports a static quenching process. The Zeta potential analysis was used to characterize the surface condition of CDs, and the change of Zeta potential could confirm the binding capacity of CDs and Cd2+ ions. As shown in Fig. 8(b), CDs showed negative Zeta potential at – 15.2 mV in water solution, resulting from the carboxylic groups present on the surface of CDs. Then, the addition of Cd2+ ions showed the value of Zeta potential increased up to −3.62 mV. This notable raising in the Zeta potential value in the presence of Cd2+ ions confirmed that the CDs was combined with the Cd2+ ions by the ligand-to-metal charge transfer [32]. Furthermore, TCSPC was carried to evaluate excitation action of CDs. The dynamic quenching process consists quenchers competing with spontaneous emission process to shorten the lifetime of excited molecules. However, for static quenching, quenchers formed non-luminance species while the lifetime of excited fluorescence molecules is stable. As shown in Fig. 8(c), time life of produced CDs was 10.78 ns, turning to 10.14 ns after addition of Cd2+. A slight change of time life strongly supported mechanism of static quenching. All these results demonstrated the formation of a close binding of fluorescent agent (CDs) and quencher (Cd2+) after addition of Cd2+.

In summary, a continuous hydrothermal method in microreactor system was developed to prepare N, S co-coped fluorescent CDs in 10 min. The obtained CDs were well separated from each other without apparent aggregation, exhibiting a shape of nearly sphere with a narrow diameter distribution of 2.44 nm − 3.43 nm at average of 2.79 nm. The synthesized CDs possess outstanding water solubility, and excellent photo properties. The CDs solution emitted strong blue fluorescence under UV light at 365 nm, achieving a high quantum yield at 68.2%, exceeding most reported work. Basing on the fact that CDs were selectively quenched by Cd2+, the CDs were applied for sensitive detection of Cd2+ with a limit of detection at 0.079 ppb with a wide linear range from 0 ppb to 300 ppb. Possible quenching mechanism has been discussed through complex analyze methods, which could be ligand–metal binding process. This report will help to provide convenient production method and enhance the mechanism understanding of advanced sensing applications.
Yu Cheng: Data curation, Writing – original draft. Zhuo Chen: Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Yundong Wang: Resources. Jianhong Xu: Supervision, Resources, Methodology, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the supports of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22025801 22108147), Shui Mu Xue Zhe of Tsinghua University (2020SM056), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2021M691761) for this work.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2022.113910.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Colorimetric fluorescent paper strip with smartphone platform for quantitative detection of cadmium ions in real samples

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H.Q. Wang, L.G. Da, L. Yang, S.Y. Chu, F. Yang, S.M. Yu, C.L. Jiang, Colorimetric fluorescent paper strip with smartphone platform for quantitative detection of cadmium ions in real samples, Journal of Hazardous Materials 392 (2020) 122506.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluoro- and Chromogenic Chemodosimeters for Heavy Metal Ion Detection in Solution and Biospecimens

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: T.Q. Duong, J.S. Kim, Fluoro- and Chromogenic Chemodosimeters for Heavy Metal Ion Detection in Solution and Biospecimens, Chem Rev 110(10) (2010) 6280-6301.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A selective fluorescent sensor for imaging Cd2+ in living cells

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X.J. Peng, J.J. Du, J.L. Fan, J.Y. Wang, Y.K. Wu, J.Z. Zhao, S.G. Sun, T. Xu, A selective fluorescent sensor for imaging Cd2+ in living cells, J Am Chem Soc 129(6) (2007) 1500-+.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Synthesis of cadmium and zinc semiconductor compounds from an ionic liquid containing choline chloride and urea

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P.J. Dale, A.P. Samantilleke, D.D. Shivagan, L.M. Peter, Synthesis of cadmium and zinc semiconductor compounds from an ionic liquid containing choline chloride and urea, Thin Solid Films 515(15) (2007) 5751-5754.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Eu(III) functionalized Zr-based metal-organic framework as excellent fluorescent probe for Cd2+ detection in aqueous environment

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X.Y. Xu, B. Yan, Eu(III) functionalized Zr-based metal-organic framework as excellent fluorescent probe for Cd2+ detection in aqueous environment, Sensors and Actuators B-Chemical 222 (2016) 347-353.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Cadmium Modifies the Cell Cycle and Apoptotic Profiles of Human Breast Cancer Cells Treated with 5-Fluorouracil

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Asara, J.A. Marchal, E. Carrasco, H. Boulaiz, G. Solinas, P. Bandiera, M.A. Garcia, C. Farace, A. Montella, R. Madeddu, Cadmium Modifies the Cell Cycle and Apoptotic Profiles of Human Breast Cancer Cells Treated with 5-Fluorouracil, Int J Mol Sci 14(8) (2013) 16600-16616.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: High sensitive gold-nanoparticle based lateral flow Immunodevice for Cd2+ detection in drinking waters

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A.M. Lopez-Marzo, J. Pons, D.A. Blake, A. Merkoci, High sensitive gold-nanoparticle based lateral flow Immunodevice for Cd2+ detection in drinking waters, Biosens Bioelectron 47 (2013) 190-198.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Determination of cadmium by flame-atomic absorption Spectrometry after preconcentration on silica gel modified with cupferron

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: G.G. Bortoleto, G.T. Macarovscha, S. Cadore, Determination of cadmium by flame-atomic absorption Spectrometry after preconcentration on silica gel modified with cupferron, J Brazil Chem Soc 15(2) (2004) 313-317.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Determination of cadmium(II) ion by atomic absorption spectrometry after cloud point extraction

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Golbedaghi, S. Jafari, M.R. Yaftian, R. Azadbakht, S. Salehzadeh, B. Jaleh, Determination of cadmium(II) ion by atomic absorption spectrometry after cloud point extraction, J Iran Chem Soc 9(3) (2012) 251-256.
O.T. Butler, J.M. Cook, C.M. Davidson, C.F. Harrington, D.L. Miles, Atomic spectrometry update. Environmental analysis, J Anal Atom Spectrom 24(2) (2009) 131-177.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: An environmentally-friendly, highly efficient, gas pressure-assisted sample introduction system for ICP-MS and its application to detection of cadmium and lead in human plasma

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y.P. Cao, B.Y. Deng, L.Z. Yan, H.L. Huang, An environmentally-friendly, highly efficient, gas pressure-assisted sample introduction system for ICP-MS and its application to detection of cadmium and lead in human plasma, Talanta 167 (2017) 520-525.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A Highly Selective Fluorescent Sensor for Distinguishing Cadmium from Zinc Ions Based on a Quinoline Platform

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X.Y. Zhou, P.X. Li, Z.H. Shi, X.L. Tang, C.Y. Chen, W.S. Liu, A Highly Selective Fluorescent Sensor for Distinguishing Cadmium from Zinc Ions Based on a Quinoline Platform, Inorg Chem 51(17) (2012) 9226-9231.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A Highly Selective On/Off Fluorescence Sensor for Cadmium(II)

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Q. Zhao, R.F. Li, S.K. Xing, X.M. Liu, T.L. Hu, X.H. Bu, A Highly Selective On/Off Fluorescence Sensor for Cadmium(II), Inorg Chem 50(20) (2011) 10041-10046.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Green preparation of carbon dots with papaya as carbon source for effective fluorescent sensing of Iron (III) and Escherichia coli

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C.E. Machado, K.O. Vieira, J.L. Ferrari, M.A. Schiavon, Carbon Quantum Dots: Chemical Synthesis, Properties and Applications, Rev Virtual Quim 7(4) (2015) 1306-1346.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Sustainable carbon-dots: recent advances in green carbon dots for sensing and bioimaging

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: V. Sharma, P. Tiwari, S.M. Mobin, Sustainable carbon-dots: recent advances in green carbon dots for sensing and bioimaging, J Mater Chem B 5(45) (2017) 8904-8924.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon quantum dots from natural resource: A review

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Das, R. Bandyopadhyay, P. Pramanik, Carbon quantum dots from natural resource: A review, Mater Today Chem 8 (2018) 96-109.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Detection of Co2+ via fluorescence resonance energy transfer between synthesized nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots and Rhodamine 6G

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Dutta, B. Rooj, T. Mondal, D. Mukherjee, U. Mandal, Detection of Co2+ via fluorescence resonance energy transfer between synthesized nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots and Rhodamine 6G, J Iran Chem Soc 17(7) (2020) 1695-1704.
